coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots with theoretical understanding for cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Xian
Yang, Dan
Wang, Liming
Wen, Zhuoqi
Cui, Zhongjie
Wang, Le
He, Haiyang
Zhang, Wanlu
Han, Zhongkang
Mei, Shiliang
Qu, Songnan
Guo, Ruiqian
coredata.dc:description: Recently, red emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) have drawn widespread attention on account of their desirable fluorescence properties and good biocompatibility. Despite great efforts, facile synthesis of R-CDs for cellular imaging remains challenging and the fluorescence mechanism of R-CDs is still elusive. Herein, p-phenylenediamine-derived R-CDs with excitation-independency were successfully obtained through a facile solvothermal approach together with proportional precipitation. The fluorescent solvatochromism of R-CDs is realized, while high polarity leads to higher degree of dipole interaction between R-CDs and different solvents, favoring for emissive red-shift. Furthermore, density functional theory is adopted to explore the optical and electronic characteristics of some polycyclic aromatic molecules. Among different configurations, pyridine nitrogen and carbonyl bonds could relatively increase the charge density and significantly narrow the band gap, which can provide a crucial theoretical basis for the precise preparation of R-CDs. Moreover, R-CDs possess favorable cellular imaging ability, which indicates their potential for a promising candidate as fluorescence probes in bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red emission
Cellular imaging
Density functional theory (DFT) calculation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) PL characterization of R-CDs dissolved in water excited by different wavelength; (b) the fitted fluorescent decay curve and (c) FT-IR spectrum of R-CDs in water.
(a) XPS spectrum of R-CDs. High-resolution (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s and (d) O 1 s spectra.

(a) Normalized PL spectra of R-CDs in five solvents excited by 400 nm; (b) linear fitting plot between ET (30) and fluorescent wavelength. 1: Acetone, 2: DMSO, 3: Ethanol, 4: EG, 5: Water; (c) digital photographs under a UV lamp (365 nm) and the specific mechanism illustration of R-CDs in five solvents.
(a) Ball-and-stick models, (b) simulated UV–vis absorption spectra; (c) HOMO-LUMO energy gaps of different aromatic molecules (S1-S6).
Calculated presentation of HOMO-LUMO for different aromatic molecules (S1-S6).
Bright-field, fluorescence and merged images of MDA-MB-231 cells stained with R-CDs. The scale bar is 100 µm.
Schematic illustration for R-CDs concerning (a) the synthetic process and (b) the endocytosis process of R-CDs entering living cells. Created with BioRender.com.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots with theoretical understanding for cellular imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, red emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) have drawn widespread attention on account of their desirable fluorescence properties and good biocompatibility. Despite great efforts, facile synthesis of R-CDs for cellular imaging remains challenging and the fluorescence mechanism of R-CDs is still elusive. Herein, p-phenylenediamine-derived R-CDs with excitation-independency were successfully obtained through a facile solvothermal approach together with proportional precipitation. The fluorescent solvatochromism of R-CDs is realized, while high polarity leads to higher degree of dipole interaction between R-CDs and different solvents, favoring for emissive red-shift. Furthermore, density functional theory is adopted to explore the optical and electronic characteristics of some polycyclic aromatic molecules. Among different configurations, pyridine nitrogen and carbonyl bonds could relatively increase the charge density and significantly narrow the band gap, which can provide a crucial theoretical basis for the precise preparation of R-CDs. Moreover, R-CDs possess favorable cellular imaging ability, which indicates their potential for a promising candidate as fluorescence probes in bioimaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are regarded as a novel class of fluorescence materials owing to their distinct attributes, including low cost, favorable water-solubility and prominent biocompatibility [1–3]. However, most reported CDs exhibit strong photoluminescence (PL) emission only in short wavelength region, which severely hinders their biomedical applications [4,5]. Red emitting CDs (R-CDs) not only have stronger penetrability into tissues or cells but cause less damage to them because of the lower energy, which is more suitable for biological applications [6].

Owing to broad range of precursors, the inherent existence of functional groups like -NH2, -CO, -OH, and -COOH results in complicated nature of CD structures, which greatly hinders further understanding and rational design for practical applications [7]. Thus, systematic research under the same series is essential for detailed understanding of structure-property relationships. The overall structure of phenylenediamine system has strong regularity, and is an excellent choice to systematically study CDs in depth. There are some reports achieving R-CDs using phenylenediamine successfully, which open up new research fields as pioneering works [8]. However, the synthesis process often contains time-consuming preparation and complicated purification methods such as silica column chromatography or acid treating [9]. Therefore, a simple strategy to fabricate CDs on the basis of phenylenediamine with red fluorescence emission is still needed.

Recently, the luminescent mechanism for CDs has been theoretically studied through the utilization of density functional theory (DFT), which could provide more information about the relationship between structure and properties. [10,11] Heteroatom doping is known as a unique method to modify the PL properties of CDs due to the involvement in synthesizing carbon structure. In this case, different nitrogen/oxygen-doping configurations could change the energy levels of CDs, which is related to the process of protonation and deprotonation [12]. The variation of charge density will finally cause the shift of emission wavelength. The role of different kinds of nitrogen and oxygen remains ambiguous to determine the emission wavelength of CDs [10]. In 2018, Mojtaba et al. calculated the electronic structure of a variety of nitrogen-containing molecules, proposing that the aromatic domain state in the multi-component nitrogen-containing heterocyclic molecule is an important emission center for CDs [13]. In 2016, Yang et al. claimed that CDs synthesized in the ethylenediamine citrate system could form a tiny IPCA (imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid, 1,2,3,5-tetrahydro-5-oxo) structure, which is considered as an independent fluorescence molecule linked with the surface/insides of CDs [14].

Our work before has realized tunable emission from green-orange based on phenylenediamine system [15,16]. The fluorescence properties of R-CDs should be further investigated based on one set of precursors so that the proposed mechanism will be more convincing. Therefore, delicate designment could be realized on purpose of more targeted application for CDs concerning with phenylenediamine system.

In this work, a facile strategy of R-CDs for cellular imaging was demonstrated. Using p-phenylenediamine and urea as precursors, the R-CDs were obtained by a solvothermal method in the environment of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) ( Scheme 1), which were precipitated by petroleum ether and ethyl acetate. To confirm the possible responsible mechanism in fluorescence properties of R-CDs, the electronic and optical characteristics of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons with oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups were investigated using DFT calculations. The as-obtained R-CDs show fine water-solubility, favorable biocompatibility and desirable imaging performance, which have the potential for bioimaging application.

The overall distribution of the R-CDs was recorded by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100 F, JEOL). The structure of R-CDs was measured by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker). X-ray photoelectron spectra were collected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250XI). PL data were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F97XP, Shanghai Lengguang). The characterization of surface functionalities was conducted by Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, Thermo Fisher). The fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs were conducted by a time-resolved spectrofluorometer (FLS 920, Edinburgh Instruments). The Zeta potential was characterized by a Zetasizer (Malvern, UK).

Through a solvothermal method, p-phenylenediamine (0.5 g) and urea (1 g) were first dispersed in DMF (15 mL). Then, a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave was adopted to provide heating environment and the precursors were heated at 180 ℃ for 6 h. After cooling down, R-CDs were purified with petroleum ether and ethyl acetate. Subsequently, the black sediment at the bottom could be collected through centrifuging at 8000 rpm. The obtained sediment was further washed by deionized water and steamed in rotation three times. Later on, the purified R-CDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying in a vacuum freeze dryer. The polyetherimide (PEI)-modified R-CDs were further synthesized through a microwave-assisted heating.

The human breast cancer MDA-MB-231 cells were seeded in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) at 37 ℃ with 5 % CO2 overnight. The PEI-modified R-CDs (25 μg/mL) were put into each well, and incubated with cells for 3 h. After being washed by phosphate buffer saline (PBS) buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7.4), the cells were ready for imaging measurements.

The molecular structures were optimized by the B3LYP/6–31 G* methods. The optimized molecular structures were simulated and energy level distribution of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) was obtained. The absorption spectra of these structures were obtained by density functional perturbation theory (DFPT). All the calculations used the Gaussian09 software. [17,18].

According to previous work, 180 ℃ is selected as the proper carbonized temperature and lower degree of carbonization could lead to more polymerized structure, which corresponds to later polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. [19,20] The luminescent characteristics of R-CDs were firstly recorded. As presented in Fig. 1a, the R-CDs in water sample exhibit a red fluorescence emission at 619 nm and show the excitation-independent performance. Furthermore, the PL decay curve of R-CDs in water is investigated as exhibited in Fig. 1b. With an average luminescent lifetime of 4 ns, the PL decay curves of R-CDs could be well fitted by a single exponential function, which indicates only one channel of energy level. The specific exponential function is shown as follows:
f
t
=
y
0
+
A
1
e
−
τ
τ
1
, (
y
0
=7.2368,
A
1
=2911 and
τ
1
=4.0). Since different energy levels correspond to the formation of various radiative transitions, abundant amino groups contribute to higher content of nitrogen with electron donation and significantly improve the passivated states of R-CDs [21]. In this case, single energy level is dominated and leads to excitation-independent emission, which is in accordance with previous work. [19], [21].

Based on FT-IR spectroscopy, the surface functionalities and bonding configurations of R-CDs were studied. According to Fig. 1c, the broad band within 3100–3600 cm−1 indicates the presence of hydroxyl bond (O-H) and imino bond (N-H), coming from the nitrogen or oxygen containing groups of the precursors. The peak at 2890 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching mode of C-H. Besides, the significant band from 1570 to 1750 cm−1 originates from the vibrations of carbonyl bond (CO) and aromatic CC. This result indicates that abundant CO bond is connected to the surface of R-CDs. Besides, the peaks locating at ∼1411 and ∼1239 cm−1 derive from the bending vibration of C-N and CN, respectively. The presence of symmetric C-O-C results in the weak peak within 990–1170 cm−1. [9], [15].

The elemental composition of R-CDs was evaluated by XPS spectra. As exhibited in the full-scan XPS spectra ( Fig. 2a), there exist three typical peaks including C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s. Besides, the atomic percentages of C, N and O are 64.09 %, 24.67 % and 11.24 %, respectively. In Fig. 2b, the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum indicate four different types of carbon including dominant graphitic sp2 C-C/CC (284.6 eV), C-N (285.4 eV), C-O (286.0 eV) and CO (289 eV) [22]. On the basis of N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2c), the peaks at 398.5 eV, 399.3 eV and 400.7 eV are indexed to pyridinic N, amino N and pyrrolic N, respectively. [11], [23] The carbocycles of p-phenylenediamine become rich-nitrogen heterocyclic structures after the high temperature and pressure treatment. It could improve the conjugation of the electronic structure together with the increased electron density, causing the lowering of band gap and a redshift in PL spectra [23]. Based on O 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2d), the two peaks fitted at 531.5 eV and 533.1 eV are ascribed to the presence of CO and C-O, correspondingly. For R-CDs, the carbonyl bonds are formed during protonation and could determine the wavelength of the emission peak [24].

In order to explore the morphology and size of R-CDs, TEM images are collected as presented in Fig. S1. It can be seen that the average diameter of the R-CDs is 4.8 nm (Fig. S1a). Besides, the HRTEM image (Fig. S1b) exhibits that R-CDs are ellipsoidal and have a good crystallinity. The lattice spacing of R-CDs is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the lattice planes (100) of graphic carbon. [16] The XRD pattern of R-CDs (Fig. S2) presents a broad peak at 23°, referring to the lattice planes (002) of graphic carbon. This result demonstrates that the obtained R-CDs are graphite-like structure. [15].

The PL spectra of R-CDs in five different solvents, containing acetone, DMSO, ethanol, ethylene glycol (EG) and water, were investigated. As presented in Fig. 3a, a tendency towards long wavelength occurs when the solvent changes from acetone to water. With the polarity of the solvents increasing, the peak position of PL spectra tuned from 539 nm to 619 nm (Table S1). Moreover, when dispersed in the five solvents, R-CDs emit different colors of fluorescence from lemon-yellow to red under a UV lamp (Fig. 3c).

In search of the effect dependent on different solvents to R-CDs, the Reichardt’s polarity parameter ET (30) is adopted. The value of ET (30) refers to the overall solvation capability with regard to the formation of nonspecific (e.g. dipole-dipole) and specific (hydrogen bond; HB) interactions for solvent molecules [25]. In Fig. 3b, the peak position and the ET (30) show a good linear relationship, which means that the fluorescence wavelength of R-CDs could be well-regulated by the solvents. Since R-CDs possess rich surface groups such as -OH, -NH2 and -COOH, the HB could form between the R-CDs and the solvents with different polarity. On the basis of aliphatic and polymeric chains, “soft” and extended functional groups on the surface endow R-CDs with the solubility in various solvents and tunability sensitive to chemical variations from external environment like pH or solvent polarity. It has been reported that the existence of steric hindrance sensitive to solvent polarity could alter the dipole interaction between carbon molecular and solvents, which presents as surface modification [26]. Upon different degree of solvent polarity, these disordered carbon atoms with sp3/sp2 hybridization embedded into carbon matrix could generate transition paths and thus alter internal charge density [27]. With the increase of the solvent polarity, the HB energy between R-CDs and the solvents becomes stronger and then the fluorescence wavelength shifts towards longer wavelength as shown in Fig. 3d [28].

The precursors p-phenylenediamine and urea provide plenty of surface groups like -COOH, -OH and -NH2. In the process of solvothermal synthesis, the solvent environment of DMF could promote the formation of a carbon-oxygen double bond due to the oxidizable aldehyde group of DMF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT calculation based on phenylenediamine system
ce:section.ce:para: Based on previous reports [15,16], the fluorescence of CDs based on phenylenediamine system could be regulated within blue-yellow region. The position of emissive wavelength could also be changed by post-treatment including solvent-dependent effect or redox reaction. From the analyst of FT-IR and XPS spectra, nitrogen/oxygen-containing groups dangling on the surface of CDs influence the fluorescence greatly. Utilizing DFT, a series of small polycyclic aromatic molecule models are designed to simulate the influence of nitrogen/oxygen-containing groups exerted on the luminescent characteristics of CDs. Typically, 6-membered fused aromatic rings are also called polyaromatic hydrocarbons consisting of purely carbon and hydrogen atoms [29]. The inherent framework of p-phenylenediamine involves with basic benzene rings and the rigorous carbonization process motivates the formation of intra- and intermolecular bonds. Since heteroatom doping triggers side-functionalization effects, the chemical nature of CDs counts greatly on atomic composition together with doping position in the structure as PL centers [29].

To investigate the influence brought by different functional groups and their different position to the electronic structure of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, six different molecule models S1-S6 are designed as shown in Fig. 4a. Coronene molecule (C24H12) containing 7 benzene rings with hydrogen terminated edges is selected as the initial model (S1), and other models (S2-S6) are obtained by changing the dopant atoms or functional groups on the basis of S1. Besides, the particle size of all the models is controlled to be similar. Model S2 is a heterocyclic ring with the introduction of a nitrogen atom at the edge of the molecule. Model S3 is obtained by continuing to introduce a nitrogen atom in model S2. Model S4-S6 are modified on the basis of Model S1 with -NH2, -OH and CO bonds. Since the atomic radius of N or O is similar to that of C, heteroatoms substituting is tolerable to the aromatic matrix and the regular π electron network is disrupted with increased reactivity.

The calculated absorption spectra of Model S1-S6 are exhibited in Fig. 4b. The absorption peak of the coronene molecule (S1) is located at 200 nm and 350 nm. The absorption peaks of S2, S4 and S5 around 200 nm are similar to that of S1, but a new peak appears in the lower-energy region. Larger deviations are observed in the absorption spectra of S3 and S6 where the absorption peaks around 200 nm have an obvious red-shift and more new peaks are observed. These results reveal that the addition of the nitrogen atom or surface functionalities to S1 will lead to the change of absorption peak to varying degrees. Among them, the introduction of the internal nitrogen atom and the CO bond could cause significant red-shifts of the absorption spectra [30].

The HOMO-LUNO energy gaps of these small molecules are also calculated by DFT (Fig. 4c). The energy gap (∆ε1) of the coronene molecule S1 is 4.03 eV. Similar to the absorption spectra, nitrogen or oxygen atoms doping will lead to a decrease of the energy gap. As for S2, S4 and S5, the addition of nitrogen atom, amino and hydroxyl at the edge will make the energy gap reduced slightly. However, the energy gap of S6 (3.31 eV) is obviously narrower than S1 owing to the introduction of CO bond. It is worth noting that a significant difference in energy gap could be seen between S3 (1.77 eV) and S1 due to the presence of internal nitrogen-containing heterocycle. In the solvothermal process of R-CDs, the participation of DMF with high polarity could help introduce the internal N atoms. From the characterization of R-CDs, the content of pyridine nitrogen dominates in the XPS N 1 s spectra and a strong peak of CO bonds is exhibited in the FT-IR spectra, which reveals that the structure of R-CDs paves the way for long-wavelength fluorescence emission.

The internal heterocyclic nitrogen atom and the oxygen atom of CO bonds contribute to the HOMO-LUMO orbital, leading to a significant narrowing of the band gap ( Fig. 5) [31]. The CDs obtained from phenylenediamine system could be considered as a sp2 hybridized graphitic network [32]. The type and content of nitrogen/ oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs could influence their optical property. Combining the first-principles calculation, the effects of doping atoms on the fluorescence performance of CDs are studied theoretically. Both the pyridine nitrogen and the CO bonds could increase the charge density, thus exhibiting long-wavelength emission.

As for the nanomaterials-biological interface, the reasonable control of the surface charge is crucial to reduce the surface energy and thus promotes the absorption of certain ions as well as biological macromolecules [33]. According to Zeta potential measurement, the surface of R-CDs is negatively charged with − 4.87 mV, which might be ascribed to the ionization of oxygen-containing groups carboxylic acid and hydroxy groups. Thus, electrostatic interaction motivates the wrapping of amino rich PEI conjugated with R-CDs [34]. As a cationic branched polymer, the repeated units of PEI equipped with active amine group could favor the attachment of R-CDs via non-covalent interaction and enhance the efficiency of cellular uptake [34]. By neutralizing some negative charges, R-CDs could come into contact with cells effectively. Furthermore, abundant amino groups endow the R-CDs with colloidal stability as well as additional adsorption sites for further modification with multiple therapeutic drugs.

Taking advantage of good biocompatibility, the capability of R-CDs for cellular imaging was studied by fluorescence microscope. For cytotoxicity evaluations, the results of CCK-8 assay are presented in Fig. S3. The cell viability after incubation for 24 h is determined to be at least 86.5 % even when the concentration of R-CDs is up to 200 μg/mL, which implies that R-CDs are biocompatible with low cytotoxicity [5]. The human breast cancer MDA-MB-231 cells are incubated with R-CDs for 3 h, respectively. Subsequently, MDA-MB-231 cells are observed under 507 nm laser excitation. Fig. 6 shows the MDA-MB-231 cells incubated with R-CDs have strong red fluorescence, which demonstrates that R-CDs could be swallowed into cells. Besides, no obvious change in the morphology of cells is observed after incubation with R-CDs, which indicates that the cells are in good condition. All these results reveal that R-CDs are capable of cellular imaging with the advantages of favorable biocompatibility and good imaging capability.

In this work, a facile one-pot solvothermal method for synthesizing R-CDs has been developed. The tuning of emission wavelengths from red to yellow was achieved based on the solvent-dependent effect, which is ascribed to the HB between the solvents and R-CDs. Furthermore, the contribution of pyridine nitrogen and the CO bonds to longer wavelength was verified from the perspective of molecular structure by DFT calculation. With favorable water-solubility and desirable biocompatibility, the R-CDs are demonstrated to serve as a favorable fluorescence probe for bioimaging.

Xian Wei: Writing – original draft, Resources, Conceptualization. Dan Yang: Writing – original draft, Resources, Conceptualization. Liming Wang: Investigation, Validation. Zhuoqi Wen: Investigation, Validation. Zhongjie Cui: Investigation, Validation. Le Wang: Methodology, Visualization. Haiyang He: Methodology, Visualization. Wanlu Zhang: Methodology, Visualization. Zhongkang Han: Software. Shiliang Mei: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Songnan Qu: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Ruiqian Guo: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 62074044), Zhongshan-Fudan Joint Innovation Center and Jihua Laboratory Projects of Guangdong Province (X190111UZ190).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2022.112869.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence-tunable carbon nanodots: surface-state energy-gap tuning
Adv. Mater.

Rogach, Hydrogen peroxide-treated carbon dot phosphor with a bathochromic-shifted, aggregation-enhanced emission for light-emitting devices and visible light communication, Adv. Sci. 5 (2018) 1800369–1800377.

Appl. Surf. Sci.

Inorg. Chem.

Mater. Today

Nano Today

Small

Nanoscale
